articles.title: 25 Iconic Lines from Anthony Head on Buffy the Vampire Slayer and Ted Lasso That Are Absolute Perfection
articles.content:
著名演员安东尼·海德去世，享年72岁。他因在90年代标志性电视剧《吸血鬼猎人巴菲》中饰演鲁珀特·贾尔斯（别名守望者），以及在近期热门剧集《足球教练》中饰演冷酷刻薄的俱乐部老板鲁珀特·曼尼恩而广为人知。在其长达45年的演艺生涯中，斯库比族粉丝（《巴菲》迷）和足球爱好者们都将深深怀念他的才华以及他在这些和其他节目中带来的冷静、沉稳的气质。

海德1954年出生于伦敦，最初从事舞台表演，后来在雀巢咖啡的一系列广告中获得演出机会，这些广告后来在美国重新拍摄为咖啡品鉴师品牌，使他因优雅迷人的风度而获得广泛关注。从那时起，他便顺利迈向那些让他成名的角色，并在此后的数十年间持续从事舞台、影视和配音工作。

以下是来自安东尼·海德两个最具标志性角色的25条幽默、讽刺且如今读来令人感伤的经典台词。失去他，电视荧屏将不再一样。正如那位永远一脸无奈的贾尔斯所说：“见鬼了。”

《吸血鬼猎人巴菲》中的最佳贾尔斯台词

- “巴菲，当我说你可以猎杀吸血鬼并且拥有社交生活时，我的意思不是同时进行。”
- “我相信这里的潜台词正在迅速……变成明文。”
- “茶能让人放松。我希望自己紧张起来。”
- “赞德，别在书本面前说拉丁语。”
- “我和斯派克一起看了《激情》。此事永远不要再提。”
- “是的，赞德，你又一次成功地把一个复杂的想法简化成了最简单的形式。”
- “涉及电脑的事物让我充满孩子般的恐惧。要是换成什么善良的食人魔之类的，我就自在多了。”
- “宽恕是一种慈悲，巴菲。这……这不是因为他们值得，而是因为他们需要。”
- “如果我想听你的意见，斯派克，我会……我永远也不会想听你的意见。”
- “呃，不。我其实不是平克·弗洛伊德乐队的原始成员。但关于怪物的事，是的。”
- “你们从我这里得到的只有我的支持。还有我的尊重。”
- “我很感激你对这个问题的看法。事实上，我鼓励你在觉得合适的时候随时挑战我。你永远不应该被权威吓倒。当然，除了在这种情况下——显然我是对的而你是错的。”
- “看在上帝的份上，她已经18岁了，而你的情感成熟度就像一个蓝莓司康饼。直接行动吧，行吗？别在那儿扭扭捏捏的。”
- “赞德，就因为这事儿永远不会成功，也没必要这么消极嘛。”
- “好吧，我就跳进我的时光机，回到12世纪让吸血鬼们推迟他们的远古预言好了……”
- “守望者蔑视重力！”
- “请停下你正在做的事。你闻起来像果乐卷。”
- “地球肯定完了。”

《足球教练》中的最佳鲁珀特·曼尼恩台词

- “请叫我鲁珀特。”后来又说：“是曼尼恩先生。”
- “哦，得了吧，萨丝。咱们就让过去的过去吧，嗯？我现在有女儿了。我变了。”
- “我想要一个孩子。我只是觉得……那时候不想要而已。”
- “如果你改变主意，说一声然后……发了。他马上到。”
- “嗯，那样的话，我就是个彻头彻尾的混蛋了，不是吗？”
- “来场比赛怎么样？我们可以赌一把……比如说，一万英镑？”
- “祝你好运。”
articles.summary:
Anthony Head, the charming, handsome and versatile actor best known for playing Rupert Giles, aka the Watcher, on the iconic ’90s television show Buffy the Vampire Slayer, and Rupert Mannion, the cruel and snarky club-team owner on the more recent powerhouse Ted Lasso, has died at the age of 72. Scoobies (Buffy fans) and soccer (er, football) aficionados alike will miss his talent and the cool, level-headed presence he brought to these and other shows over his 45-year career.

Born in 1954 in London, Head started out on stage before landing a gig in a series of Nescafé commercials, later reshot in the U.S. for Taster’s Choice, which brought him widespread recognition for his suave and debonair demeanor. From there, it was a short leap to the roles that made him famous, and he continued doing stage, screen and voiceover work for decades.

Ahead, we have 25 hilarious, wry and—now that he’s gone—bittersweet quotes from two of Anthony Head’s most iconic characters. Television just won’t be the same without him. To quote the ever-exasperated Giles: “Bloody hell.”

Get Reader’s Digest’s Read Up newsletter for more entertainment news, humor, travel, tech and fun facts all week long.

Best Giles quotes from Buffy the Vampire Slayer

  • “Buffy, when I said you could slay vampires and have a social life, I didn’t mean at the same time.”
  • “I believe the subtext here is rapidly becoming, uh, text.”
  • “Tea is soothing. I wish to be tense.”
  • “Xander, don’t speak Latin in front of the books.”
  • “I watched Passions with Spike. Let us never speak of it.”
  • “Yes, Xander, once more you’ve managed to boil a complex thought down to its simplest possible form.”
  • “Things involving the computer fill me with a childlike terror. Now if it were a nice ogre or some such, I’d be much more in my element.”
  • “To forgive is an act of compassion, Buffy. It’s … it’s not done because people deserve it. It’s done because they need it.”
  • “When I want your opinion Spike, I’ll … I’ll never want your opinion.”
  • “Well, no. Um, I wasn’t actually one of the original members of Pink Floyd. But about the monster stuff, yes.”
  • “All you will get from me is my support. And my respect.”
  • “I appreciate your thoughts on the matter. In fact, I encourage you to always challenge me when you feel it’s appropriate. You should never be cowed by authority. Except, of course, in this instance when I am clearly right and you are clearly wrong.”
  • “For God’s sake, man, she’s 18, and you have the emotional maturity of a blueberry scone. Just have at it, would you, and stop fluttering about.”
  • “Xander, just because this is never going to work, there’s no need to be negative.”
  • “All right. I’ll just jump into my time machine, go back to the 12th century and ask the vampires to postpone their ancient prophecy …”
  • “A watcher scoffs at gravity!”
  • “Please stop whatever you’re doing. You smell like Fruit Roll-Ups.”
  • “The Earth is definitely doomed.”

Best Rupert Mannion quotes from Ted Lasso

  • “Please, call me Rupert.” Later: “It’s Mr. Mannion.”
  • “Oh, come on, Sass. Let’s let bygones be bygones, eh? I’ve got a daughter now. I’ve changed.”
  • “I want a child. I just suppose I didn’t want one … then.”
  • “If you change your mind, say the word and … sent. He’ll be right over.”
  • “Well, now, that would have made me a real piece of sh—, wouldn’t it?”
  • “How ’bout a game? Maybe we could wager … say, £10,000?”
  • “Good luck.”

RELATED:

  • David Boreanaz Is Heading Back to Primetime In NBC’s Rockford Files Reboot
  • Stranger Things Is Over—Now What?! Here Are 7 Shows to Watch to Fill the Void
  • Still Chasing That Euphoria High? These 8 Shows Are Just as Addictive

At Reader’s Digest, we’re committed to producing high-quality content by writers with expertise and experience in their field in consultation with relevant, qualified experts. We rely on reputable primary sources, including government and professional organizations and academic institutions as well as our writers’ personal experiences where appropriate. We verify all facts and data, back them with credible sourcing and revisit them over time to ensure they remain accurate and up to date. Read more about our team, our contributors and our editorial policies.

articles.title: You Won’t Believe How Queen Elizabeth Got People to Open Up at Dinner Parties
articles.content:
你有没有想过参加奢华皇室晚宴究竟是什么感觉？我们一直很好奇。是的，照片里看起来确实光鲜亮丽，但我们一直怀疑那会很拘谨、很正式——说实话，还有点古板。嗯，看来我们大错特错了！

虽然还没有人（暂时）透露与查尔斯国王共进晚餐的真实情况，但他已故母亲伊丽莎白二世女王主持的宴会据说相当有趣。其中一个可能的原因是什么？据最接近她工作的人透露，女王有一个非常聪明（而且出人意料地不皇室！）的方式来保持餐桌上的对话流畅，许多客人透露的内容可能超出了他们原本的意图。

那么女王是如何让客人们如此坦诚地交谈的呢？我们给你一个提示：这个技巧我们很多人可能也非常熟悉。继续往下看，看看你的猜测是否正确。

订阅《读者文摘》的Read Up新闻通讯，获取本周关于皇室、幽默、清洁、旅行、科技和有趣事实的更多内容。

在我们介绍她略带狡黠的派对策略之前，我们应该先了解一下伊丽莎白女王在位70年间主持和出席了多少活动。她每年负责几场常规皇室活动，包括夏季的四场皇家花园派对、国宴和一些王宫招待会。虽然很难确定她所有社交活动的确切数字，但我们知道她在其统治期间共欢迎了150多场国宴的客人。

说到出席活动，这些数字就迅速攀升了。例如，她的工作安排在2016年达到峰值——332场公务活动——是的，仅那一年！之后，由于疫情和她年事已高，这些数字有所下降。2022年，她生命的最后一年，她参加了大约120场活动。

陛下让饮品不断流动！据为她服务了35年的管家朱利叶斯·史密斯说，女王确保客人们的酒杯不断续满。如果有人用手捂住酒杯，委婉地阻止再次斟酒，女王会指示服务生继续倒酒。史密斯回忆道：“她会让人把酒从他们的手指缝里倒进去。”

唯一的出路？看来就是慢慢喝了。毕竟，直接对君主说“不”会被认为是不礼貌的（当然也违反了严格的皇室礼仪规则）。我们只能想象有多少人在宴会结束时带着微醺离开，可能第二天都记不清发生了什么——以及他们对女王透露了什么。

有人可能会争辩说这可能是她让高级别客人泄露所有（国家）秘密的方式。但史密斯说有一个更简单、更接地气的原因：“她过去喜欢八卦。”

苏格兰前首席部长尼古拉·斯特金基本上证实了女王对所有“内幕消息”的渴望，她声称这位已故君主对政治八卦非常着迷，总是试图获取头条新闻背后的“真实故事”。当然，对幕后发生的事情感到好奇并不意味着女王自己传播八卦。事实上，她被认为非常谨慎，是一个始终将个人意见藏在心里的女人。

私下里，她相当有调皮的一面。据为女王工作了近二十年的皇室助理萨曼莎·科恩说，这位君主不仅有顽皮的幽默感，还秘密地“喜欢事情出错”——在她众多正式公务活动中。“如果蛋糕切不开或者匾牌揭不开，因为一切都安排得如此完美，当事情出错时，她的生活反而有了调味。”科恩透露道。

在私下场合，女王不需要隐藏她的笑声。据报道，她是皇室圣诞派对上搞笑礼物的忠实粉丝，当哈里王子在她的手机上留下一个调皮的信息恶作剧她时，她笑得前仰后合。

她确实喜欢喝酒。在某个时候，有传言说女王每天享受四杯酒，其中一杯是她最喜欢的两成分鸡尾酒，她每天中午之前都会喝。不过，了解她的人很快澄清了事实，说她实际上并没有过“酗酒”的生活方式。她的前厨师达伦·麦克格雷迪在接受CNN采访时回应小报报道时说：“如果她喝那么多，早就醉倒了。”而史密斯说得稍微圆滑一些：“她肯定不会……把喝酒作为日常习惯。”

女王的前管家俏皮地告诉《每日邮报》，女王母亲才是家族中最能喝的人。据皇室内部人士称，伊丽莎白女王（2002年以101岁高龄去世）每周喝70个酒精单位。这大约相当于35小杯烈酒或31杯葡萄酒！据报道，她每天喝两杯凯歌粉红香槟，还有各种葡萄酒和烈酒。

现任君主偶尔被看到喝酒，但他不算重度饮酒者。他喜欢葡萄酒以及金汤力，但他最喜欢的饮品据说是马提尼。他的朋友蒂博尔·卡尔诺基伯爵透露，多年前当他访问罗马尼亚时，查尔斯每次晚餐前都会喝一杯。趣闻：查尔斯似乎总是用自己的鸡尾酒杯，那是他出国旅行时自己装在包里带去的。

王位继承人威廉王子似乎也偶尔享受一杯酒。2026年6月访问伦敦的佩科姆王子酒吧时，他宣称自己“喜欢酒吧”，并补充说：“我想尽我所能支持它们，因为我喜欢这种社区氛围。……我们需要保护我们的酒吧。”大多数普通人大概不会反驳这一点。

《读者文摘》发布了数百篇关于英国皇室的故事，提供了对君主制迷人方面的幕后观察。我们定期报道包括最新皇室新闻、悠久传统的历史和意义，以及每个人喜爱的皇室成员的日常怪癖，从伊丽莎白女王每日的小吃到威廉王子关于家庭生活的坦白。我们致力于由具有专业领域专业知识和经验的作家与相关合格专家协商制作高质量内容。我们依靠可信的主要来源，包括政府和专业组织以及学术机构，以及作者在适当情况下的个人经历。我们验证所有事实和数据，用可靠来源支持，并随着时间的推移重新审视，以确保它们保持准确和最新。了解更多关于我们团队、贡献者和编辑政策的信息。
articles.summary:
Have you ever wondered what it’s really like to attend a fancy royal dinner? We certainly have. Yes, it always looks gorgeous in pictures, but we’ve always suspected it would be very prim, very proper and—let’s be honest—a little stuffy. Well, it seems we were very wrong!

Nobody has spilled the beans (yet) on what dinner parties with King Charles are like, but the ones hosted by his late mother, Queen Elizabeth II, were apparently pretty spirited. One of the likely reasons? According to those working closest to her, the queen had a very clever (and surprisingly unroyal!) way to keep the conversation going at the table, with many guests revealing more than they’d probably intended to.

So how did the queen get her guests talking so openly? We’ll give you a hint: It’s a trick many of us are probably pretty familiar with too. Read on to see if you guessed right.

Get Reader’s Digest’s Read Up newsletter for more royals, humor, cleaning, travel, tech and fun facts all week long.

How many events did Queen Elizabeth host in any given month?

Before we get to her slightly naughty party strategy, we should note just how many events Queen Elizabeth both hosted and attended over the course of her 70 years on the throne. She was in charge of a couple regular royal events every year, including four royal garden parties in the summer, as well as state banquets and a handful of palace receptions. While it’s hard to pinpoint exact numbers for all her soirees, we know that she welcomed guests to more than 150 state dinners in total during her reign.

When it came to attending events, those numbers balloon quickly. For example, her schedule peaked in 2016 with 332 engagements—yes, just in that one year! After that, the numbers went down due to both the pandemic and her advancing age. In 2022, her final year, she went to roughly 120 events.

What was her secret tactic for getting people to open up?

Her Majesty kept the drinks flowing! According to Julius Smith, her butler of 35 years, the queen made sure her guests’ wine glasses were constantly topped off. And if someone put their hands over their glasses to discreetly prevent yet another refill, the queen would instruct her servers to keep pouring. “She would say to pour it in through their fingers,” Smith recalled.

The only way out? Drinking very slowly, it seems. After all, telling the monarch a straightforward “no” would have been considered impolite (and certainly a breach of the strict royal etiquette rules). We can only imagine how many people must have left the table tipsy at the end of the night, probably not remembering half of what had happened—and what they had revealed to the queen—the following day.

Why did she do this?

One could argue it might have been her way of making high-profile guests spill all their (state) secrets. But Smith says there was a much simpler, and more relatable, reason: “She used to love to gossip.”

Her predilection for wanting all the tea was more or less confirmed by Scotland’s former first minister Nicola Sturgeon, who claimed that the late monarch was obsessed with political gossip and always tried to get the “real story” behind the headlines. Of course, being curious about what was going on behind the scenes didn’t mean that the queen spread gossip herself. In fact, she was considered exceptionally discreet and a woman who’d keep her personal opinions to herself at all times.

In private, she had quite a mischievous side, though. According to Samantha Cohen, a royal aide who worked closely with the queen for nearly two decades, the monarch not only had a cheeky sense of humor but also secretly “loved it when things went wrong” during her many official engagements. “If a cake was not cutting or a plaque didn’t unveil, because everything was so perfectly organized, it spiced her life up when things went wrong,” Cohen revealed.

Behind closed doors, the queen didn’t need to hide her giggles. She was reportedly a big fan of the joke gifts at the royal Christmas party and was in stitches when Prince Harry pranked her with a cheeky message on her phone.

Did Queen Elizabeth enjoy imbibing herself?

She certainly did. At some point, it was rumored that the queen enjoyed four drinks a day, one of them being her favorite two-ingredient cocktail, which she drank before noon every day. That being said, those who knew her were quick to set the record straight and say that she did not actually lead a “boozy” lifestyle. Addressing the tabloid reports, her former chef Darren McGrady told CNN that “she’d be pickled if she drank that much,” while Smith phrased it slightly more diplomatically, saying, “She certainly wouldn’t be drinking … as part of the routine.”

The queen’s ex-butler cheekily told the Daily Mail that it was the queen mother who was the heaviest drinker in the family. That Queen Elizabeth, who died at the age of 101 in 2002, drank 70 alcohol units a week, according to royal insiders. That equals roughly 35 single shots or 31 glasses of wine! She reportedly drank two glasses of Veuve Clicquot pink champagne per day, along with different wines and spirits.

Does King Charles drink?

The current monarch has been spotted having a drink occasionally, but he is not considered a heavy drinker. He likes wine as well as gin and tonic, but his favorite drink is reportedly a martini. His friend Count Tibor Kalnoky revealed that he had one every night before dinner when he visited him in Romania years ago. Fun fact: Charles apparently always drinks from his own cocktail glass, which he brings in his bag when traveling overseas.

Prince William, next in line to the throne, also seems to enjoy a drink once in a while. When visiting the Prince of Peckham pub in London in June 2026, he declared that he “loves pubs,” adding, “I want to do as much as I can to support them because I love the community. … We need to protect our pubs.” Most commoners probably wouldn’t argue with that.

Reader’s Digest has published hundreds of stories on the British royal family, providing a behind-the-scenes look at the fascinating facets of the monarchy. We regularly cover topics including the latest royal news, the history and meaning behind time-honored traditions, and the everyday quirks of everyone’s favorite family members, from Queen Elizabeth’s daily snack to Prince William’s confessions about his home life. We’re committed to producing high-quality content by writers with expertise and experience in their field in consultation with relevant, qualified experts. We rely on reputable primary sources, including government and professional organizations and academic institutions as well as our writers’ personal experiences where appropriate. We verify all facts and data, back them with credible sourcing and revisit them over time to ensure they remain accurate and up to date. Read more about our team, our contributors and our editorial policies.

articles.title: Is It Really Rude to Send a Voice Memo Instead of a Text?
articles.content:
我的朋友是Z世代，她很喜欢语音消息。她发语音消息的方式，就像别人发“哈哈哈”一样——反射性的、频繁的，而且似乎完全不在意后果。而我呢，有着1940年代电报员的灵魂。我希望我的沟通是整洁的、打字的、可编辑的。所以很自然，我们陷入了一种奇怪的技术僵局。

事情是这样的：她录一条语音消息发给我。我用通讯应用把它转录成文字，然后阅读。接着我用语音输入功能回复她，但发送的是文字，因为我喜欢在消息发出之前能够修改用词。她收到我的文字后，让手机读给她听。所以我们基本上是在用各自喜欢的格式进行完整的对话，就像某种数字版的联合国谈判。如果其中一方再引入第三种语言，比如普通话，那才叫更蠢。

我对语音消息的核心反对意见是什么？文字就是文字。它本该被阅读，在你自己的时间、以你自己的速度，理想情况下还能同时做其他三件事。一旦它需要耳机、一个安静的房间和我的全部注意力，感觉就像在工作。还有，我真的很不爱听自己的声音。

但尽管我承认自己对语音消息有点小烦恼，它们算是礼仪问题吗？发语音备忘录而不是文字，真的很失礼吗？既然我写礼仪专栏，我觉得有职业义务在某人崩溃之前搞清楚这个问题。

订阅 Reader's Digest 的 Read Up 新闻通讯，每周获取更多礼仪、幽默、清洁、旅行、科技和有趣的事实。

语音消息已经成了一个现象。根据2025年12月发布的 Statista 数据，现在每天发送94亿条 WhatsApp 语音消息——比过去三年增长35%——而且增速还在加快。超过一半的受访者认为语音消息甚至正在取代现实中的互动，Z世代中这一比例上升到60%。为了证明这一点，Snapchat 用户在2025年第一季度发送的语音消息比去年同期多出25亿条。这不仅仅是年轻人的现象：根据一项较早的 Vox/YouGov 调查，62%的美国人发送过语音消息——那是2023年的数据，所以现在这个数字肯定更高了。语音消息似乎正在朝着这个方向发展，这意味着我正在成为一个过时的老顽固。

而且，热爱语音消息的理由实际上相当充分。即使是我也能承认这一点。语音能够传达语气，而文字根本做不到。我的朋友给我发语音消息不是因为她想给我添麻烦；她说是因为这感觉更像真正的对话，更亲密。你可以听到某人什么时候在笑、什么时候压力大、什么时候对某事非常开心，而一串表情符号根本无法完全复制这些。此外，2024年的一项 Preply 调查发现，半数语音消息用户认为这种格式实际上加强了他们的关系。在真人关系正受到技术冲击的时代，这感觉意义重大。

公平地说，对于发送者来说，它们也很高效。如果你正在开车、锻炼，或者你有太多话想说又不想打一大段文字，一条45秒的语音消息就能搞定。我们已经接受了不同代际之间有着截然不同的短信风格——从婴儿潮一代像写信一样签名，到阿尔法世代完全用表情包交流——所以也许（可能）语音消息只是这场演变的下一章。

我的核心抱怨是：语音消息在形式上是异步的，但在需求上却是同步的。换句话说，你可以像发文字一样发送它们，但我得像接电话一样听它们——而就像电话一样，我没法在开会时、健身时、排队结账时，或者除了戴着耳机坐在沙发上以外的任何地方听。

说到电话，至少语音信箱有保持简短的社会契约。语音消息没有这样的限制。它们可以一直继续。没有什么——我是说真的没有什么——比收到一条四分钟的语音消息更让人无语了，当你只是想确认周六的安排是否还作数。四分钟！那是 TED 演讲。那是完整的新闻节目。那足够让我深刻重新考虑我们的友谊了。而且不同于文字——即使在最烦人的情况下，文字通常也会促使人们轮流回复——语音消息本质上是一集私人播客，没有内置的停止压力，听众也无法插嘴或引导。它是有意设计的单向对话。

文字还会自动提供书面记录——当你需要回头查找地址、寻找对方提到的东西、核对聚会细节，或者（就像我和我配偶有时发生的那样）用作争吵时的证据时，这很有用。当然，你可以回溯你所有的语音消息来找到那个信息，但谁有那个时间呢？我就派对迟到好了，谢谢。还有一个关键数据：55%的人承认他们完全忘记了要听语音消息，88%忘记了消息的内容，这是前述 Statista 调查的结果。

此外，我应该坦白，录制语音消息让我很焦虑。我会录三四遍试图听起来像个正常人，但我总是讨厌自己的声音。当我说出来，这表明真正的问题可能在我自己。

大多数时候，语音消息只是个人习惯，而不是完全的礼仪违规。但有些情况下，它们会从轻微烦人变成真正的失礼。

给一个告诉你他们不喜欢语音消息的人发送，这是大问题。如果一个人已经委婉地——甚至通过你应用的转录功能以被动攻击的方式——告诉你语音消息不适合他们，你继续发送就不再是偏好冲突了，而是无视。（相关：如果你是持续忽略别人的文字而期望他们适应你的格式，那会造成同样的不平衡动态。）

语境也很重要。给专业联系人或新认识的人发送一条三分钟的闲聊语音消息就太过分了，因为这种格式的亲密感与这段关系的正式性不匹配。而且绝对不要、任何情况下都不要用语音消息传达坏消息。那不是亲密，那只是让人本就艰难的一天更加艰难，同时他们还得手忙脚乱地找耳机。

时机也是一个重要因素。就像不接电话而用文字回复可能在某些情况下有问题一样，在明显需要快速来回回复的对话中丢一条语音消息也是一种沟通错位。比如：快速连续的“你快到了吗？”交流、需要回头查阅的简短数据交换，或者需要快速回复的群聊。看看场合……至少看看聊天记录。

如果你发现自己像我一样，两个完全合理的人站在语音消息辩论的两端，好消息是这确实可以在不进行正式调解的情况下解决。关键是在它变成沉默怨恨之前说出来。像这样的文字：“嘿，我很喜欢收到你的消息，但我通常在没法听的地方，所以回复慢一点可以吗？”这不是对抗。这是一种沟通偏好，友善而微妙地表达出来，并且自带解决方案。

除了我确实这么做了，我的朋友点头了（隐喻性的——实际上她为此发了一条语音消息给我），然后继续发语音消息。原来这对她的大脑来说效果更好，如果她不能给我发语音消息，她基本上就不跟我说话了。所以现在我得问自己：这个小小的不便值得失去一段多年的友谊吗？当然不值得。我爱她！而且说实话，这个妥协让我多花的时间可能就几分钟。

例外情况是当消息内容重要而转录完全错误的时候，这时候我得找个安静的角落，像个成年人一样去听，然后接受这就是我现在的生活。反过来，她也必须接受她的语音消息有时会几个小时不被阅读/收听，而我在等待一个安静的时刻，而且我的回复不会总是即时的。我们都成长了。

用文字回复语音消息也是完全可以的，不用感到内疚。你不必匹配格式。对等的沟通是关于深思熟虑的回应，而不是镜像传递方式。重要的是你最终确实回复了，而且你在努力找到一个中间点。我很少用语音消息回复她，这没关系。

语音消息本身并不失礼。它们是私人的、温暖的，而且越来越主流——有真实数据和真实理由支撑。它们在亲密感方面的论点是合理的。我的朋友喜欢它们并没有错；她只是在用自己的方言交流，任何关系的一部分就是学会翻译。

但这种适应真的应该双向进行。如果你是一个热衷的语音消息发送者，偶尔和生活中的人确认一下，确保他们真的在享受这种体验，而不是像联合国口译员那样带着认命的能量默默转录你的独白，这值得一试。如果你收到它们，你可以温和地、友善地要求一种更适合你的格式。

归根结底，沟通只有在真正连接两个人时才有效。而现在，我和我的朋友正通过一个稍微有点离谱的接力系统实现完全连接，这个系统涉及两个 AI 助手和相互妥协的精神。

说真的，这可能是我见过的2026年最奇葩的爱的语言了。

你有没有一个无法停止反复思考的社交情况？给我们发邮件至 advice@tmbi.com，或者在 Instagram 上给 Charlotte 发消息 @CharlotteHiltonAndersen。

Reader's Digest 已经发布了数百篇礼仪故事，帮助读者在不断变化的世界中驾驭沟通。我们定期涵盖各种话题，比如任何场合发送的最佳信息、看起来礼貌实际上不那么礼貌的习惯、电子邮件和短信礼仪、商务礼仪、小费礼仪、旅行礼仪等等。我们致力于由具有专业领域知识和经验的作家在相关合格专家的咨询下制作高质量内容。我们依赖包括政府和专业组织、学术机构以及我们作家个人经历在内的可靠一手来源。我们验证所有事实和数据，用可信来源支撑，并随着时间推移进行回顾，以确保它们保持准确和最新。了解更多关于我们团队、我们的贡献者和我们的编辑政策。

这篇文章《发语音备忘录而不是文字真的很失礼吗？》首次发表于 Reader's Digest。
articles.summary:
My friend is Gen Z, and she loves voice notes. She sends them to me the way other people send “lol”—reflexively, constantly and without apparent concern for the consequences. I, on the other hand, have the soul of a 1940s telegram operator. I want my communication tidy, typed and editable. So naturally, we’re locked into a weird technological stalemate.

Here’s how it plays out: She records a voice note and sends it. I use my messaging app to transcribe it into text, which I then read. Then I compose my reply by voice-dictating it into my phone but sending it as a text, because I like being able to fix my words before they launch into the world. She receives my text and has her phone read it to her out loud. So we’re basically having a full conversation entirely in each other’s preferred formats, like some kind of digital United Nations negotiation. The only thing that would make this dumber is if one of us introduced a third language, perhaps Mandarin.

My core objection to voice notes? A text is text. It is meant to be read, on your own time, at your own speed, ideally while doing three other things simultaneously. The moment it requires earbuds, a quiet room and my full attention, it feels like work. Also, I just really don’t like the sound of my own voice.

But while I will freely admit to being mildly annoyed by voice notes, are they a breach of etiquette? Is it actually rude to send a voice memo instead of a text? Since I write an etiquette column, I feel professionally obligated to figure this out before one of us snaps.

Get Reader’s Digest’s Read Up newsletter for more etiquette, humor, cleaning, travel, tech and fun facts all week long.

The case for voice notes (yes, I’m going to defend them)

Voice notes have become a whole thing. According to Statista data released in December 2025, 9.4 billion WhatsApp voice notes are now sent every single day—a 35% jump over the last three years—and things are accelerating. More than half of those polled feel voice notes are even replacing IRL interactions, rising to 60% among Gen Z. Proving this point, Snapchat users sent more than 2.5 billion additional voice notes in just the first three months of 2025 than they did the year prior. And this isn’t purely a young-person phenomenon: According to an older Vox/YouGov survey, 62% of Americans overall have sent a voice message—and that was in 2023, so that number is certainly higher now. Voice notes seem to be trending in that direction, which means I am becoming an outdated curmudgeon.

And look, the reasons to love them are actually pretty solid. Even I can admit that. Voice carries tone in a way that text simply can’t. My friend doesn’t send me voice notes because she’s trying to inconvenience me; she sends them because she says it feels more like a real conversation, more intimate. You can hear when someone is laughing, stressed or absolutely delighted about something, and no string of emojis fully replicates that. Plus, a 2024 Preply survey found that half of voice-note users feel the format actually strengthens their relationships. And in a time where real human relationships are under technological attack, this feels significant.

They’re also, in fairness, efficient for the sender. If you’re driving, exercising, or you just have a lot to say and don’t feel like typing a novel, a 45-second voice note gets it done. We already accept that different generations have wildly different texting styles—from Boomers who sign their texts like letters to Gen Alphas who communicate entirely in memes—so maybe (probably) voice notes are just the next chapter in that evolution.

The case against voice notes (here’s where I get to complain)

Here’s my core grievance, and I suspect it’s shared by the 30% of Americans who told Preply they find receiving voice notes annoying or inconvenient: Voice notes are asynchronous in format but synchronous in demand. In other words, you can send them like a text, but I have to listen to them like a phone call—and like a phone call, I can’t listen while I’m in a meeting, at the gym, standing in line at the store or pretty much anywhere that isn’t my couch with my earbuds in.

And speaking of phone calls, at least a voicemail has the social contract of being kept short. Voice notes have no such guardrails. They can go on. And on. There is nothing—and I mean nothing—quite like seeing a four-minute voice note arrive in your inbox when all you wanted to know was whether you’re still on for Saturday. Four minutes! That’s a TED Talk. That’s a full news segment. That’s enough time for me to deeply reconsider our friendship. And unlike a text—which, even at its most aggravating, generally nudges people to take turns—a voice note is essentially a private podcast episode, with no built-in pressure to stop and no way for the listener to interject or redirect. It’s a one-sided conversation by design.

Texts also automatically provide a written record—useful for when I need to go back and look up an address, find something the other person referenced, check party details or (as sometimes happens with my spouse) use as proof in an argument. Sure, you could go back through all your voice notes to find that info, but who has the time for that? I’ll just be late to the party, thanks. And here’s the kicker: 55% of people admit they forget to listen to voice notes altogether, and 88% forget what the message even contained, according to the aforementioned Statista survey.

Also, I probably should confess that recording a voice note gives me a lot of anxiety. I’ll do three or four takes trying to sound like a normal human, and I always end up hating the sound of my own voice. Which, when I say it out loud, suggests the real problem here might be me.

When it crosses the line

Most of the time, voice notes are a personal quirk rather than a full-on etiquette violation. But there are situations where they tip from mildly annoying to actually rude.

Sending them to someone who has told you they don’t enjoy them is the big issue. If a person has let you know—gently, or even passive-aggressively via your app’s transcription feature—that voice notes don’t work for them, continuing to send them anyway is no longer a preference clash. It’s dismissiveness. (Related: If you’re consistently ignoring someone’s texts and expecting them to work around your format, that creates the same lopsided dynamic.)

Context matters too. Sending a chatty three-minute voice note to a professional contact or a new acquaintance is a lot, because the intimacy of the format doesn’t match the formality of the relationship. And never, under any circumstances, send a voice note to deliver difficult news. That’s not intimacy; that’s just making someone’s hard day even harder while they fumble to find their earbuds.

Timing is also an important factor. Just as declining to answer a phone call and texting back instead can be problematic depending on the context, lobbing a voice note into a conversation that clearly calls for quick, back-and-forth responses is its own kind of communication mismatch. Think: a rapid-fire “are you almost here?” exchange, a quick exchange of data that people will need to refer back to later, or a group thread where people need fast answers. Read the room … or at least the chat thread.

What to do when your communication styles just don’t match

If you find yourself in a situation like mine, where two perfectly reasonable people have landed on opposite ends of the voice-note debate, the good news is that this is genuinely resolvable without a formal mediation session. The key is saying something before it becomes a silent resentment situation. A text like “Hey, I love hearing from you, but I’m usually somewhere I can’t listen, so is it OK if it takes me a bit longer to respond?” is not a confrontation. It’s a communication preference, stated kindly and subtly, with a built-in solution.

Except I did exactly that, and my friend nodded (metaphorically—she actually sent me a voice note about it) and then kept sending voice notes anyway. Turns out it just works better for her brain, and if she can’t voice-note me, she kind of stops talking to me. So now I have to ask myself: Is this glitch worth losing a yearslong friendship over? Of course it’s not. I love her! And honestly, the compromise costs me maybe a few extra minutes.

The exception is when the message is something important and the transcription gets it hilariously wrong, at which point I have to find a quiet corner, listen like an adult and accept that this is my life now. She, in turn, has had to accept that her voice notes will sometimes sit unread/unlistened to for hours while I wait for a moment of silence and that my replies will not always be immediate. We have both grown as people.

It’s also completely acceptable to respond to a voice note with a text and not feel guilty about it. You don’t have to match the format. Reciprocal communication is about responding thoughtfully, not mirroring the delivery method. What matters is that you did eventually respond and that you’re trying to meet each other somewhere. I very rarely return her message with a voice note, and that’s OK.

The verdict

Voice notes are not inherently rude. They are personal, warm and increasingly mainstream—backed by real numbers and real reasons. The intimacy case for them is legitimate. My friend isn’t wrong to love them; she’s just communicating in her own dialect, and part of any relationship is learning to translate.

But that adaptation really should go both ways. If you’re a devoted voice-note sender, it’s worth checking in occasionally with the people in your life to make sure they’re actually enjoying the experience and not silently transcribing your monologues with the resigned energy of a UN interpreter. And if you receive them, you’re allowed to gently, kindly ask for a format that works better for you.

At the end of the day, communication is only good if it actually connects two people. And right now, my friend and I are achieving full connection through a slightly unhinged relay system that involves two AI assistants and the general spirit of mutual compromise.

Which, honestly, might be the most 2026 love language I’ve ever seen.

Have a social situation you can’t stop ruminating on? Email us at advice@tmbi.com, or message Charlotte on Instagram at @CharlotteHiltonAndersen.

Why trust us

Reader’s Digest has published hundreds of etiquette stories that help readers navigate communication in a changing world. We regularly cover topics such as the best messages to send for any occasion, polite habits that aren’t as polite as they seem, email and texting etiquette, business etiquette, tipping etiquette, travel etiquette and more. We’re committed to producing high-quality content by writers with expertise and experience in their field in consultation with relevant, qualified experts. We rely on reputable primary sources, including government and professional organizations and academic institutions as well as our writers’ personal experiences where appropriate. We verify all facts and data, back them with credible sourcing and revisit them over time to ensure they remain accurate and up to date. Read more about our team, our contributors and our editorial policies.

Sources:

  • GlobeNewswire: “Heineken Launches New WhatsApp Technology That Swaps Voice Notes for Real Life Conversations Over a Beer”
  • Marketing Dive: “Heineken swaps voice notes for beer in latest bid for IRL connections”
  • Snapchat: “The Snapchat Generation Report: Where Culture Gets Real”
  • NPR: “Are you getting more voice notes these days? You’re not alone”
  • Preply: “Voice notes may be the new phone call, according to 40% of Americans”
  • Mediapost: “Snapchat Users Sending Billions More Voice Notes”
  • WRAL: “Life coach: Why teens are talking more—and typing less”

Is It Rude to Have ChatGPT Write Your Emails?

The post Is It Really Rude to Send a Voice Memo Instead of a Text? appeared first on Reader's Digest.

articles.title: This Pandemic-Era Trend May Have Finally Reached Full Saturation—Here’s the Hobby That’s Taking Its Place
articles.content:
新冠疫情期间，你是否培养了新爱好？许多人被困在家中，或只与固定伙伴小范围外出，急切寻找打发时间的方式。我们尝试着去年认为不可能的事情（比如说织毛衣），只想做点别的，而不是看书或发呆。虽然那个时代可能已经过去，但那些爱好保留了下来——其中一项爱好曾以比进家门时摘口罩还快的速度迅猛发展。

但根据土地信托基金会（TPL）的一份新报告，有一种爱好的热度可能正在回落，尽管此前堪称火箭式飙升。你可能会嗤之以鼻，因为了解到它仍然非常流行。但数据就是数据，接下来我将带你了解土地信托基金会的最新报告以及其他研究机构的调查数据。看看哪种爱好可能已经达到了最大饱和度。

这就是——匹克球！在疫情期间，我们许多人迫切想要到户外活动。我们被困在家中，健身房关闭，面包做得多到自己都需要发泄情绪的出口（对于那些没穿运动裤的人来说，可能还需要消耗腰围）。于是，匹克球这项此前鲜为人知的运动被我们疯狂追捧。目前，美国有超过82000个匹克球场，根据美国匹克球协会的数据。相较之下，2019年，长期研究集团的一份报告显示当时仅有18000个球场。

令人震惊的是，这种令人上瘾、易上手（虽然精通不易）且广受喜爱的社交运动似乎正在走下坡路。根据土地信托基金会的数据，2026年，美国人口最多的100个城市中新建的匹克球场数量仅比去年增长4%。相比之下，2024年增长14%，2025年增长13%（虽仍火热但已在减弱），你会感觉这个趋势可能已经触顶。

美国匹克球协会在其“匹克球爱好者”数据库中追踪全美新建匹克球场地的情况，数据显示更为显著。2024年至2025年间，该协会记录的新建场地数量下降了47%——从2024年的4000个降至2025年的2300个。当然，美国匹克球协会和土地信托基金会追踪的指标不同，但总体上都表明美国公众对这项运动的热情有所减退。

目前有三种户外活动正在挑战匹克球的地位：园艺、飞盘高尔夫和“户外健身区”（稍后会解释，因为我们也不知道那是什么）。看来人们仍然喜欢到户外享受大自然，即使去匹克球场挥拍的人减少了。

根据土地信托基金会的报告，园艺支出增长了8%，飞盘高尔夫（也就是用飞盘打球的高尔夫）也比去年增长了4%。园艺当然是一种历史悠久的爱好，既能放松身心，又能美化家居外观。飞盘高尔夫是较新的潮流，但它同样满足了人们待在户外的渴望，同时为喜欢竞争和社交互动的人提供了切磋的对象。

户外健身区是指在当地公园常见的迷你健身房。它们就像是加州健美运动员开创的著名室外“肌肉海滩”健身区的缩小版，人们可以使用引体向上杆、平衡木以及其他器械进行力量和有氧训练。

不！全美有82000多个匹克球场，仅美国100个最大城市就有超过3700个（比2017年增长了900%），匹克球依然蓬勃发展。随便找个周日（或周六、周二）去当地公园，你一定能听到多个匹克球被击打时发出的独特“啪啪”声。根据体育与健身行业协会的数据，2025年有超过2300万人打匹克球——比2020年增长了479%——所以，除非飞盘高尔夫球场和户外健身房大量涌现，否则看来匹克球将继续存在。
articles.summary:
Quick question: Did you pick up a new hobby during the COVID-19 pandemic? Many of us, stuck inside or venturing out sparingly with those in our chosen pod, were desperate to find something to fill our time. We were trying things we thought impossible the year before (hello, knitting), just to do anything except read a book or stare off into the middle distance. Well, that era may be behind us, but the hobbies stayed—including one that took off faster than your mask when you walked through the front door.

But according to a new report from the Trust for Public Land (TPL), one hobby in particular might be coming back down to earth after what can only be described as a meteoric rise. You might scoff once you learn what it is, since it’s still wildly popular. But numbers are numbers, and ahead, I’ll take you through the latest TPL report as well as data from other research and polling. Read on to see which hobby may have already reached peak saturation.

Get Reader’s Digest’s Read Up newsletter for more news, cleaning, humor, travel, tech and fun facts all week long.

Which big trend is now past its prime?

It’s pickleball! During the pandemic, many of us were clamoring to get outside and do something. We were stuck at home, gyms were closed, and we were making so much homemade bread that we really needed an outlet for our pent-up emotions (and waistlines, for those of you not wearing sweatpants). Enter pickleball, a previously little-known sport that we took to like crazy. Right now, there are more than 82,000 pickleball courts in the U.S., according to USA Pickleball. Compare that to 2019, when a report from sports research company Longitudes Group put the number at 18,000 courts.

Shockingly, the addictive, easy-to-play (if not to master) and beloved social juggernaut appears to be on the downhill slide. In 2026, the number of new pickleball courts in the top 100 most populous U.S. cities was up just 4% from last year, according to the TPL. Compare that to 14% growth in 2024 and a still-feverish (but waning) 13% in 2025, and you get the feeling this trend might have already hit its peak.

USA Pickleball, which tracks new pickleball court locations across the U.S. in its “Pickleheads” database, shows even starker recent numbers. Between 2024 and 2025, the org reported a 47% decrease in the number of new pickleball locations added to their database—from 4,000 in 2024 to 2,300 in 2025. Of course, USA Pickleball and the TPL track different metrics, but overall they both indicate a little less rabid enthusiasm for the sport among the American public.

What hobby is taking its place?

Three outdoor activities are currently giving pickleball a run for its money: Gardening, disc golf and “outdoor fitness zones” (which will get an explanation coming up, since we didn’t know what those were either). It seems like people are still interested in getting outside and enjoying nature, even if fewer of us are clop-clopping our way around the pickleball courts.

According to the TPL report, garden spending is up 8%, and disc golf, aka golf with frisbees, saw a 4% bump from last year. Gardening, of course, is an age-old hobby that’s both relaxing and boosts our home’s curb appeal. Disc golf is a newer craze, but it likewise satisfies our urge to be outside while giving those who like a little competition and social interaction some people to spar with.

Outdoor fitness zones are those mini-gyms that you sometimes see at your local park. They’re like scaled-down versions of the outdoor “Muscle Beach” gyms made famous by California bodybuilders, where people can use pull-up bars, balance beams and other equipment for resistance and aerobic workouts.

Does this mean we won’t be seeing pickleball anymore?

Nope! With 82,000-plus courts around the U.S. and more than 3,700 in America’s 100 biggest cities alone (a 900% jump from 2017), pickleball is alive and well. Just head down to your local park on any given Sunday (or Saturday or Tuesday) and you’ll no doubt hear the distinctive thwack thwack of multiple pickleballs being knocked around. According to the Sports & Fitness Industry Association, more than 23 million people played pickleball in 2025—up 479% since 2020—so unless there’s a massive influx of frisbee golf courses and outdoor gyms, it seems like pickleball is here to stay.

  • Everyone’s Talking About the World Cup—Here’s How This Summer’s Biggest Event Will Be Uniquely American, for Better and for Worse
  • Flesh-Eating Bacteria Could Kill Your Summer Plans—Here’s Whether You Should Be Worried About Going to the Beach
  • This Simple Trick Will Stop Seagulls from Stealing Your Food at the Beach (And It’s 100% Sillier Than You’re Imagining)

Why trust us

At Reader’s Digest, we’re committed to producing high-quality content by writers with expertise and experience in their field in consultation with relevant, qualified experts. We rely on reputable primary sources, including government and professional organizations and academic institutions as well as our writers’ personal experiences where appropriate. We verify all facts and data, back them with credible sourcing and revisit them over time to ensure they remain accurate and up to date. Read more about our team, our contributors and our editorial policies.

Sources:

  • Trust for Public Land: “City Park Facts”
  • Axios: “Pickleball craze peters out”
  • USA Pickleball: “Annual Growth Report”
  • Longitudes Group: “Longitudes Group Makes Available Its First Pickleball Distribution Report”
  • Michigan State University: “What is an outdoor fitness park”
  • Sports & Fitness Industry Association: “U.S. Pickleball Participation Statistics”
